text.title: API Gateway Patterns & Technology Selection Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every api-gateway-patterns application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Use simple routing when the architecture has a direct mapping between client requests and backend services with minimal transformation needed and a single client type or uniform client needs.
rule.rationale: Simple routing avoids operational overhead of BFF or aggregation patterns. Adding patterns prematurely creates maintenance burden without benefit.

rule.statement: Use BFF when different clients have vastly different data needs, mobile needs optimized payloads, third-party integrations require different contracts, or different security requirements exist per client type.
rule.rationale: A single gateway serving all clients tends to accumulate client-specific logic and becomes a bottleneck; BFF pushes that complexity to per-client surfaces owned by the teams that know those clients.

rule.statement: Use aggregation when reducing client-side complexity is a primary goal, when minimizing network round trips matters (mobile, low-bandwidth), or when creating composite API views from independently deployed services.
rule.rationale: Without aggregation, clients make N parallel requests; mobile clients on slow links feel latency multiplied by the number of services. Aggregation at the gateway trades gateway complexity for client simplicity.

rule.statement: Use edge+mesh when services require mutual TLS between them, when fine-grained traffic management between services is needed, or when a zero-trust security model is required. Do NOT use a service mesh for a simple two-tier app — the operational overhead is substantial.
rule.rationale: The mesh handles east-west (service-to-service) while the gateway handles north-south (client-to-gateway). Combining them avoids duplicating concerns at both layers.

rule.statement: Every gateway selection MUST document at least one rejected alternative with a one-sentence reason. Selections without rejected alternatives are blocked.
rule.rationale: Without rejected alternatives the ADR is decision laundering and provides no audit value.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
